name: Schelling Focal Point Governance
executive_summary:
Schelling Focal Point Governance (SFPG) applies Thomas Schelling's concept of focal points -- equilibria that players converge on through tacit reasoning about shared context rather than explicit negotiation -- to the problem of open-source software governance. In Schelling's original formulation, two people who must meet in New York without communicating will both go to Grand Central Station at noon, not because it is objectively optimal but because it is salient: both know that both know it is the obvious choice. SFPG argues that successful open-source projects achieve coordination at scale not primarily through explicit governance mechanisms (voting, committees, benevolent dictators) but through the strategic deployment of salient conventions that serve as focal points for contributor behavior. CONTRIBUTING.md files, semantic versioning, conventional commit formats, and standard directory structures function as coordination devices that allow thousands of independent contributors to align their behavior without centralized direction.

The framework distinguishes between 'natural' focal points (conventions that emerge organically from shared technical culture, such as placing source code in a /src directory) and 'engineered' focal points (conventions deliberately chosen and promulgated by project maintainers to channel contributor behavior). SFPG predicts that projects whose governance relies on widely-recognized standard conventions (engineered focal points drawn from the broader ecosystem) will achieve faster contributor onboarding and lower coordination overhead than projects that invent idiosyncratic conventions, even when the idiosyncratic conventions are equivalently documented. This is because standard conventions benefit from pre-existing salience: a new contributor already knows what semantic versioning means, so the convention coordinates behavior before the contributor reads any project-specific documentation.

SFPG also identifies a class of governance failures it terms 'focal point fractures' -- situations where a project's conventions lose their salience, typically because the conventions were ambiguous in a novel situation, because a schism in the contributor community creates two competing focal points, or because the broader ecosystem shifts and the project's conventions become non-standard. Fork events in open-source projects are often preceded by focal point fractures where contributors can no longer tacitly coordinate on how to resolve a governance question. The framework predicts that projects with explicit escalation procedures for focal point fractures (meta-conventions for resolving convention ambiguity) will experience fewer forks and governance crises than projects without such meta-conventions.

mapped_components.unit: A single contributor decision: how to format a commit message, where to place a new file, how to name a function, whether and how to propose a breaking change. Each decision is a coordination game where the contributor must choose an action that aligns with what other contributors expect, without explicit communication about each specific decision.
mapped_components.variation: The range of possible conventions available for each coordination problem: commit message formats (conventional commits, free-form, ticket-referenced), versioning schemes (semver, calver, ad hoc), directory structures (framework-standard, project-specific, flat), and governance processes (lazy consensus, formal voting, BDFL decree). Each convention has a different salience level depending on how widely it is recognized in the broader ecosystem.
mapped_components.selection: Conventions are selected by their ability to serve as focal points: conventions that are salient (widely recognized), unambiguous (clearly applicable to the specific decision), and self-reinforcing (each use increases future salience) outcompete alternatives. Selection occurs through contributor behavior -- the convention that most contributors independently adopt without explicit coordination becomes the de facto standard.
mapped_components.fitness: Convention fitness is measured by coordination efficiency: the rate at which new contributors produce correctly-formatted, correctly-placed, correctly-scoped contributions without requiring explicit correction from maintainers. Higher fitness conventions reduce the ratio of coordination overhead to productive contribution.
mapped_components.boundary: SFPG applies to communities where contributors operate independently and must coordinate through shared expectations rather than direct instruction. It is less applicable to small teams (under ~10 people) where direct communication is feasible for every decision, or to organizations with formal hierarchical authority where conventions are enforced rather than adopted. The theory assumes contributors share enough cultural context to recognize salience, which may fail in highly cross-cultural open-source communities.

falsifiable_predictions: Projects adopting widely-recognized standard conventions (semantic versioning, conventional commits, standard directory structures from popular frameworks) will show lower median time-to-first-accepted-contribution for new contributors than projects with idiosyncratic conventions of equivalent documentation length and quality, controlling for project complexity and language ecosystem.

When a project migrates from an idiosyncratic convention to an ecosystem-standard convention (e.g., adopting conventional commits after using a custom format), the rate of correctly-formatted contributions from first-time contributors will increase within 90 days, even before the new convention is fully documented in the project's CONTRIBUTING.md.

Open-source project forks will be statistically preceded by focal point fractures -- measurable as a spike in governance-related issue discussions, competing convention proposals, or CI failures due to convention violations -- in the 6-12 months before the fork event. Projects without such fractures will fork at significantly lower rates.

Projects that explicitly define meta-conventions for resolving convention ambiguity (e.g., 'when the style guide is silent, follow the most popular convention in the ecosystem' or 'the RFC process resolves convention disputes') will experience 40-60% fewer governance crises (measured by maintainer turnover, extended governance debates, or contributor exodus events) than comparable projects without meta-conventions.

Cross-ecosystem convention adoption will follow a power-law distribution: a small number of conventions (semver, MIT/Apache licensing, .gitignore patterns, README.md structure) will be adopted by the vast majority of projects, while the long tail of alternative conventions will each be adopted by very few projects. This distribution is a signature of focal point dynamics rather than optimality, since many alternative conventions are technically equivalent.

Contributor surveys will reveal that the primary reason for adopting project conventions is 'it is what everyone does' or 'it seemed obvious' rather than 'the documentation told me to' or 'I evaluated alternatives.' This tacit-reasoning pathway is the defining feature of focal point coordination as distinct from rule-following.

supporting_evidence.finding: Schelling's original experiments (1960) demonstrated that people reliably converge on focal points in pure coordination games with no communication. In the classic New York meeting problem, the majority chose Grand Central Station at noon. In number-picking games, the majority chose '1' or round numbers. The convergence rate far exceeded chance, demonstrating that salience is a real coordination mechanism independent of explicit rules.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Provides the foundational experimental evidence that focal point coordination works in practice. SFPG extends this from one-shot games between pairs to iterated coordination among thousands of independent contributors, arguing that the mechanism scales because salience is a property of the convention itself rather than pairwise negotiation.
supporting_evidence.source: Schelling, T.C. (1960). The Strategy of Conflict. Harvard University Press.

supporting_evidence.finding: The adoption of semantic versioning (semver.org) across open-source ecosystems followed a pattern consistent with focal point dynamics: early adoption by a few high-visibility projects, rapid convergence as the convention became salient, and eventual near-universal adoption not because semver was objectively optimal but because it became the convention everyone expected. Package managers (npm, Cargo, pip) reinforced the focal point by building tooling around semver assumptions.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Demonstrates SFPG's prediction that convention adoption follows focal point dynamics rather than optimality evaluation. Semver has well-known limitations (it cannot capture all meaningful change types), but its salience as a coordination device outweighs its technical imperfections.
supporting_evidence.source: Preston-Werner, T. (2013). Semantic Versioning 2.0.0. semver.org; widespread ecosystem adoption documented in package manager specifications.

supporting_evidence.finding: Fogel's 'Producing Open Source Software' (2005, revised 2017) documents that successful open-source projects converge on similar governance patterns not because of explicit copying but because the same solutions are 'obvious' to experienced open-source participants. Projects that deviate from these patterns typically experience higher contributor friction and governance disputes, consistent with the cost of deviating from focal points.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Provides practitioner evidence that open-source governance functions through tacit convergence on salient patterns. Fogel's observation that similar governance structures emerge independently across projects is the hallmark of focal point coordination.
supporting_evidence.source: Fogel, K. (2017). Producing Open Source Software: How to Run a Successful Free Software Project, 2nd edition. O'Reilly Media.

supporting_evidence.finding: Eghbal's 'Working in Public' (2020) documents how GitHub's platform design (issues, pull requests, CONTRIBUTING.md templates, standardized CI badge locations) created a set of ecosystem-wide focal points that reduced coordination costs for new contributors across all hosted projects. The platform essentially manufactured salience by making certain conventions structurally prominent.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Demonstrates 'engineered focal points' at the ecosystem level. GitHub's platform conventions function as coordination devices that allow contributors to transfer tacit knowledge across projects, exactly as SFPG predicts.
supporting_evidence.source: Eghbal, N. (2020). Working in Public: The Making and Maintenance of Open Source Software. Stripe Press.

experimental_designs.name: Convention Salience and First-Contribution Latency
experimental_designs.description: Sample 200 GitHub repositories matched by language, size, star count, and documentation quality but varying in convention standardness (measured by a scoring rubric: does the project use conventional commits, semver, standard directory structure, GitHub issue/PR templates, standard linting configurations?). For each repository, measure the median time from a new contributor's first repository interaction (issue comment, fork) to their first accepted pull request. Control for project complexity (cyclomatic complexity, test coverage requirements), community responsiveness (median time to first maintainer comment), and documentation volume.
experimental_designs.hypothesis: Projects in the top quartile of convention standardness will show 25-40% shorter first-contribution latency than projects in the bottom quartile, even after controlling for documentation volume. The effect will be largest for contributors who have previously contributed to other projects in the same ecosystem (who can transfer focal point knowledge).
experimental_designs.feasibility: High. All data is available through the GitHub API and GHTorrent/GH Archive datasets. The convention standardness rubric requires manual validation on a subset but can be automated for most dimensions. Sample size of 200 is achievable with language-stratified matching.

experimental_designs.name: Focal Point Fracture and Fork Prediction
experimental_designs.description: Build a longitudinal dataset of 50 major open-source project forks (including Node.js/io.js, OpenOffice/LibreOffice, MySQL/MariaDB, etc.) and 100 matched non-fork controls. For each project, code the 12 months before the fork (or matched time period for controls) for indicators of focal point fracture: governance-related issue volume, convention-violation CI failures, competing RFC proposals for the same problem, maintainer public disagreements about convention interpretation, and contributor sentiment in discussion threads. Test whether a composite focal-point-fracture score predicts fork events.
experimental_designs.hypothesis: Fork events will be preceded by a 2-3x increase in focal-point-fracture indicators relative to baseline levels and relative to matched controls. The fracture score will predict fork events with AUC > 0.75, outperforming simpler predictors like contributor count decline or maintainer burnout indicators alone.
experimental_designs.feasibility: Moderate. The main challenge is building a comprehensive fork dataset and finding appropriate controls. Coding governance discussions requires trained human raters for a subset, though NLP-based sentiment and topic classification can augment coverage. Historical data availability varies by project.

limitations: SFPG may overstate the role of tacit coordination relative to explicit governance in large, well-funded open-source projects. Projects like Linux, Kubernetes, and Chromium have extensive formal governance structures (committees, SIGs, release processes) that function through explicit authority rather than focal point convergence. The theory may apply more to mid-sized community projects than to corporate-backed mega-projects.

The concept of 'salience' is difficult to operationalize and measure independently of the convergence behavior it is supposed to explain. If salience is inferred from adoption rates, the theory risks circularity: 'people converge on salient conventions' becomes 'people converge on conventions that people converge on.' An independent measure of salience (perhaps based on convention age, ecosystem prevalence, or platform prominence) is needed to make the theory genuinely predictive.

SFPG assumes a shared cultural context among contributors that makes the same conventions salient to everyone. In increasingly global open-source communities, conventions that are salient to North American or European developers may not be salient to contributors from different technical cultures. The theory needs to account for how focal points work (or fail) across cultural boundaries.

The framework may underweight the role of tooling enforcement. Many conventions are adopted not because they are salient but because CI pipelines reject non-conforming contributions. This is enforcement, not coordination -- the linter does not care about salience. SFPG needs to distinguish between conventions maintained by focal point dynamics and conventions maintained by automated enforcement, as they have different failure modes.

Focal point theory predicts convergence but does not predict which focal point will win when multiple salient options compete. In cases like tabs-vs-spaces or GPL-vs-MIT, both options are highly salient, and the theory does not explain or predict which wins in a given community. The theory is better at explaining coordination success than predicting coordination outcomes.

connections_to_other_frameworks.relationship: OVPF's DOPE parameters could benefit from becoming Schelling focal points for public health policy. If DOPE-based venue classification became the standard coordination framework for pandemic response worldwide, jurisdictions could coordinate restriction policies without explicit bilateral negotiation -- exactly the focal point mechanism SFPG describes.

Both frameworks address the architecture of heterogeneous systems. SFPG argues that governance structures benefit from layered conventions (standard ecosystem conventions as the base, project-specific conventions as the specialization); nacre composites achieve toughness through layered material properties. Both predict that uniform approaches are suboptimal.

Conventions in SFPG function analogously to mordants in MFFM: the same contributor effort, applied through different conventional frameworks, produces qualitatively different outcomes. Switching from an idiosyncratic convention to a standard one transforms the 'color' of the contribution experience without changing the underlying 'dye' (code content).

Focal point fractures in SFPG share structural features with ADT's model-reality divergence. When a project's conventions lose salience, contributors' mental models of 'how we do things' diverge from each other and from reality, producing coordination failures analogous to ADT's detection failures.
